description: Registry of every web game built by gh-game-factory. Never duplicate an entry — check name, description, category, and genre before building.

games.name: unstrung
games.description: A word game about MAKING the board rather than reading it. One long strand of letters; cut out a contiguous run that spells a word, and fresh thread is spliced straight into the gap. The two joins it leaves are SEAMS - permanent, visible - and A WORD THAT SPANS A SEAM SCORES TRIPLE. So every cut chooses what the strand can spell next, and the seam you leave is the one your opponent gets to use, which makes taking the small word instead of the big one a real decision from move one. The free play is always available (the fresh thread is a short word) but it is the cheap one; the points are in the bridges. Everyone gets the same number of cuts. Three modes with genuine spread: Strand 26/min3, Deepcut 30/min4 (no filler - a position can dry up and you cut to PLACE a seam), Twinfold two 14-letter strands (never stuck, only ever choosing which to spend). DOM-rendered tile game - drag or tap-first-then-last, plus a full keyboard path; no D-pad. Solo, vs the machine, a UTC daily strand, async seed-share, and live P2P 2-4 that is LOCKSTEP rather than a snapshot star: after the deal there is zero randomness and zero hidden info (strand, spool and every refill word are pure functions of the seed), so peers exchange only {round,ply,s,i,len} and NO BOARD STATE EVER CROSSES THE WIRE - which makes host transfer purely the turn clock. THE BALANCE SIM, BUILT FIRST, OVERRULED THE DESIGN FIVE TIMES. (1) The first build was not a game: a strand you simply consume lasted 3.7 CUTS, seat 0 won 90-95%, and the signature seam rule fired on 6-12% of cuts because the board never survived long enough to accumulate joins - all invisible to unit tests. (2) The obvious fix (cap the cut length) took seat 0 from 90% to 64% of decided games and STOPPED, because the real killer was that the ply count is frequently ODD so seat 0 literally takes one more cut than seat 1 - a missing rule, not a bias; hence a fixed number of cuts EACH. (3) Refilling from the tail rotted the board: 21% of 2P and up to 100% of 4P rounds ended DRY, because cuts happen where words are, so the head became a graveyard of stubs no cut would ever clear; splicing fresh thread in AT the cut took the seam share 17% -> 46% at a stroke. (4) The fix that measured PERFECTLY was a dead game - splicing back a word as long as the one cut scored 0% dry, 0% blowouts and seats 38.0/39.5, and 23% DRAWS rising to 100% at seamMult 1, because both players took a six-letter word every turn and were handed another; no outcome-safety metric could see it, the draw rate could. (5) Snake draft, the textbook first-pick fix, OVERSHOT, and so did rotating the start seat: neither removed the 4P bias, snake moved it to the far end and enlarged it (24/28/27/17 -> 15/22/25/33) and rotate collapsed seat 0 to 13% - proving the residue is not made of pick order at all. What carries the balance is the SEAM MULTIPLIER, a design result rather than a dial (a seam is something the PREVIOUS player made, so paying triple to bridge it is a transfer from whoever moved before you), pinned by its own sweep: x1 -> seat 0 wins 77%, x2 -> 63%, x3 -> 52%. Final, replicated across 3 independent seed families at n=400: 2P ~51/47, 3P within 4 points of chance, blowouts 0-2%, dry 0%, leader-after-one-cut-each 55-60% rising to 100% by the end. Honest weak point, recorded rather than tuned away: 4P seat 3 lands ~18-19% against a 25% share and replicates. The MECHANISM AUDIT (principle #21) re-derives every rule from its OWN constants and never imports scoreOf/spansSeam - 0 violations across 9 mode x seat cells, MUTATION-VERIFIED three ways (perturbing the score formula turns 18 red, changing seam-span semantics 9, emptying the refill 18). A dictionary-shape assertion in that suite is what caught a two-hour bug where the 'guaranteed short word' refill was an EMPTY STRING because the source list started at four letters - the board dried out and every outcome metric merely read 'short rounds'. Two further defects were caught by gates nothing else could see: SOLO HAD NO SCOREBOARD AT ALL (seats were only populated on the multiplayer path, and jsdom renders an empty HUD without complaint); and POINTERDOWN HIT-TESTED WITH elementFromPoint, which returns NULL IN A BACKGROUND TAB - in the two-tab smoke test the guest's taps were silently dropped with the board visible, the HUD saying 'Your turn' and a clean console, indistinguishable from a frozen game. pointerdown now reads event.target, pinned by tests/input.test.ts which runs in jsdom where elementFromPoint is absent; mutation-verified (reverting turns 4 tests red). 308 tests.
games.status_note: Verified in a real browser on the LIVE production https URL at a true 375x812 for ALL THREE modes (Strand 26 letters/4 rows, Deepcut 30/5 rows, Twinfold 2x14/4 rows): 45px tiles, zero horizontal overflow, zero overlaps, the board fits vertically, the tile is the top element at board centre, no stray [hidden] layer, footer hidden mid-round, zero console errors, and a real cut landed through the actual input path in each. Real-pixel contrast probe on production agrees with the palette test: seam bar 8.67:1, tile 13.53:1, glyph 13.8:1. Full solo round played end-to-end through synthetic pointer events (12 cuts, one of which - DUEL - bridged a seam and paid 48 = 4^2 x 3), reaching a results screen that showed every cut and the solver's 551-point best line. Two-peer P2P smoke test run against the byte-identical local build: create-room; a typed LOWER-CASE code with stray spaces resolving to the same room; host stickiness (the creator kept HOST even though the guest's peer id sorted LOWER, which is exactly the steal the epoch model exists to prevent, and the guest never showed a host badge); the host's mode (Twinfold) travelling frozen to a guest whose own local pick was Strand; the QR panel rendering dark-on-light, square, with its quiet zone, encoding the invite LINK rather than the bare code, inside the lobby card with no overflow; in-sync play verified BOTH ways; and HOST-LEAVE -> survivor promoted at EPOCH 2, still on a live board, with its clock then auto-playing for the departed seat and the turn returning. NOT live-verified: the survivor playing all the way to the results screen (each auto-play waits out a 45s turn clock, so a full round takes ~9 minutes of real time) and the rematch vote. Both are covered by automated tests instead - takeover.test.ts asserts the promoted peer reaches game over with a full result for BOTH players and is MUTATION-VERIFIED (making setHost a no-op turns 3 red), plus rematch.test.ts, no-deadlock.test.ts, host-election.test.ts and net-lifecycle.test.ts.

games.name: undercut
games.description: Race strategy with NO driving and no tapping: the race simulates forward at a watchable pace and your only verb is the pit call — box now, onto which tyre. Every place you gain or lose is attributable to a decision. The core is the UNDERCUT vs the OVERCUT (pit early onto fresh rubber and leapfrog a rival on old tyres, or stay out on a dying set and hope the rain comes). A deterministic tyre/fuel/weather sim: per-compound wear with a quadratic CLIFF past a per-set life, a TRACK-TEMPERATURE window that shifts which compound is fastest per seed (softs love a cool track, hards need heat), fuel that burns down, and a CALIBRATED BERNOULLI rain forecast — a probability p is drawn and shown, rain is sampled ~Bernoulli(p), and the displayed p FIRMS toward the truth as the window nears, so reading the firming forecast beats pre-committing and a 40% forecast rains ~40% of the time. Three modes with genuine spread: Sprint (24 laps, dry, pure undercut/overcut), Grand Prix (40, a coin-flip rain forecast rewrites the race), Deluge (52, volatile weather that can dry back + a safety car). DOM broadcast-timing dashboard (no D-pad — a strategy game whose input is a tap on a decision). Solo-first with a pre-race GRID choice of starting tyre; async seed-share; and live P2P VERSUS 2-6 as a parallel same-seed race (each peer runs its own deterministic race over its own calls, ranked by total time, exchanging only status pings + pit events + a final result, so there is no shared state to desync and host transfer is a display concern). THE BALANCE SIM (dominant-strategy gate, principle #18) WAS BUILT FIRST AND OVERRULED THE DESIGN: the initial model had Sprint at 100% one-plan ('start medium, one-stop late onto softs') and the forecast was pure FLAVOUR because reacting to actual rain always beat pre-empting it. Two structural fixes earned the balance, not dial-twiddling: (1) TRACK TEMPERATURE, which makes the optimal start+strategy vary by seed (cold->soft-heavy, hot->hard-heavy) and broke the dry monoculture; (2) a ONE-LAP REACTION LAG (a reactor drives a lap in real water before it can box, using last lap's felt wetness), which makes a confident forecast worth pre-empting. Also caught: a same-compound pit (a fresh set of the SAME tyre — soft->soft) was silently ignored by a `call !== compound` guard, so the whole soft-soft cold-track plan scored 0% and ate the mandatory-stop penalty; fixed so any non-null call is a pit. Final at n=500/mode: no fixed strategy wins >45% and >=3 take >=10% (Sprint top ~38%, GP ~38%, Deluge ~30%), the best plan when it RAINS differs from when it stays DRY (proving the forecast changes the optimal call, not flavour), and the hindsight-optimal that can pre-empt onto wets beats the best dry-only line by ~40s/race on rain seeds. Mechanism audit (principle #21) re-derives physical invariants from INDEPENDENT constants — deg monotonic+cliff, wet/dry tyre orderings, fuel monotonic, temperature effects, forecast calibration — never the game's own arithmetic vs itself. Contrast gate holds every mark >=3:1 on every surface with the five compounds distinguished by letter+luminance+hue, not colour alone. 127 tests. In-browser verified at 375px across ALL THREE modes (zero overflow, controls >=44px, play surface top-most, overlays display:none, footer hidden mid-round, zero console errors); rain rendering confirmed on a seeded wet Deluge (field switches to wets, wrong-tyre red alert fires) and the retrospective-optimal line shows on the results screen.

games.name: boxbox
games.description: A pit-stop TRIAGE game where the decision is the game and the tapping is only the execution: a car comes in every stop with partial telemetry across four systems (tyres, fuel, wing, brakes) and a subjective, sometimes-wrong driver radio, and you choose which jobs the crew does — every job costs seconds NOW (a longer stop), every fault left costs seconds across the stint (a car carrying a problem is slow), and a system sent out CRITICAL blows up (puncture/brake failure/running dry) for a big fixed penalty, so a full service is never right and neither is the minimum. Then you run the stop: a dependency-ordered tap sequence (jack up before wheels off, wheels on before jack down, GO only when clear; an unsafe release is a penalty not a loss). Lowest total time wins; the results show the retrospectively PERFECT line on your exact seed (a per-system exact search, since the cost model decomposes per system). Live P2P VERSUS 2-6 on one shared seed + async seed-share + solo-vs-AI-field: each peer runs the identical deterministic race and exchanges only a status ping + a final result, so there is no shared continuous state to desync and host transfer is a display concern (the promoted survivor already holds every result and publishes the ladder). Chose versus over the idea's crew-roles co-op because cross-player physical dependencies deadlock the whole crew the instant one peer drops mid-stop; the co-op shape is logged in EXPANSION_IDEAS with the peer-leave reassignment + watchdog it would need. THE BALANCE SIM PROVED BOTH IDEA GATES (n=300/mode, mutation-verified): (1) triage beats raw 'do everything' tapping by ~15s Sprint / ~24s Enduro / ~13s Monsoon, winning >70% of paired races — without this it is a twitch test with a story painted on it; (2) the radio is worth hearing — weighing it halves catastrophes and saves ~1.5s in Monsoon where sensors go dark, while staying neutral where telemetry is reliable, because the radio bump scales with telemetry UNCERTAINTY (tiny when the gauge is live, large when the sensor is dark) rather than blindly obeying a driver who is wrong ~1 in 4. A first radio bot that obeyed the driver even against a live gauge was NET NEGATIVE in Sprint/Enduro (over-fixing on false alarms); the fix was the uncertainty-scaled weight. THE MECHANISM AUDIT (principle #21) re-derives every cost rule from its OWN independent constants, not the game's penaltyFor — the first version imported penaltyFor and compared the game's arithmetic against itself, a tautology that stayed GREEN when the penalty formula was mutated; the independent audit catches it (mutating penalty by +0.01 turns 3 audits red). Three modes with real spread: Sprint 4/clear-data, Enduro 7/compounding, Monsoon 5/sensors-dark. DOM-rendered (dashboard + tappable stations, no D-pad). 115 tests. Full live two-peer P2P smoke test passed against the byte-identical local build: mesh formed, host stickiness (creator kept HOST when guest joined; guest never showed a host badge), host's mode (Monsoon) travelled frozen, in-sync progress pings, and HOST-LEAVE -> survivor promoted -> kept playing to the finish -> published the ladder showing the departed host as 'left', match tally incremented.

games.name: rootbound
games.description: A boardless hex duel built on one rule: every piece from both players forms ONE colony that may never split. You win by filling every cell around the enemy Heartroot — with pieces of EITHER colour, so the rival walling themselves in does half your work and your last attacker is a piece you would rather have kept free. The signature mechanic is POLARITY: range is not a property of a piece but of where it stands — a piece among mostly its own colour is nested and moves exactly one cell, a piece outnumbered by the enemy is reaching and moves its full range, so retreating is slow and overextending is fast. Six piece types (Burrower squeezes gaps nothing else fits, Spindle leaps a line, Warden pins what it touches, Sporeling converts an enemy where it lands). Lockstep P2P for 2, not a snapshot star: zero randomness and zero hidden info mean peers exchange only {from,to,piece} and NO board state ever crosses the wire, so the desync surface does not exist and host transfer is a display concern (the promoted peer already holds a byte-identical board and just picks up the clock). THE BALANCE SIM OVERRULED THE DESIGN SEVEN TIMES across thirteen sweep rounds. Baseline was 52.5%/17.5% with 30% draws — one seat winning 3x as often, behind a green suite and a playable game. (1) The leading hypothesis, written down before testing so it could be scored, was WRONG AND INVERTED: bigger hands took thicket's draws from 29% to 92%. (2) A `heartrootBy` rule swept at 1/2/3/4 returned BYTE-IDENTICAL results at every setting because the search never delays its Heartroot — the rule never fired, so the sweep measured nothing; deleted rather than shipped unmeasured (sporeline's exact failure mode). (3) `witherSkew` measured FLAT on two modes of three, mechanistically: a skew can only compensate THROUGH the wither, and thicket's decided too few games to have purchase. (4) `openingBonus` overshoots by >2x — ~60 points of seat swing against a ~25 point problem, turning 62/38 into 32/68. (5) A FINDING TAKEN UNDER AN OLD RULE SET HAD TO BE RE-TAKEN: wider hands were rejected in round 1, but re-tested after the draw rule changed the same change took bramble's blowout rate from 47.9% to 2.1% — the single biggest quality win. (6) Bramble's wither is a PARITY CLIFF not a dial (83/17 at skew -2, 24/76 at skew 0, and firing it earlier made the cliff STEEPER, refuting the mechanism proposed one round before); it ships with no wither. (7) `tieToSecond` was not correcting bramble's bias, it WAS the bias — bramble splits 36/32/32 on its own and handing those draws to a seat manufactured a 36/64 rout out of a fair game; replaced by a seat-neutral countback on commitment. What worked: doubleRing 'mover' (draws to 0%), the wither (ring finishes 40%->76%, zero new state — a pure function of the ply counter so P2P sync was untouched), and deadwood reaching five VIA the wither instead of starting there (leader-at-move-8 67-80% -> 35%). Final at n=600 across three independent seed families: thicket 51.5/48.5 with a leader curve of 50/50/74, deadwood 48.5/51.5, bramble 55.3/44.7 of decided. Two real defects were caught by gates unit tests could not see: the CONTRAST TEST caught the two player colours at 1.07:1 luminance — the same shade of grey, while the plan beside them claimed a lightness split, i.e. turntide's shipped bug reproduced from scratch and caught on the first run; and the IN-BROWSER PASS caught a total hang where the solo bot never moved, because clearTimeout+setTimeout poked by a faster poll cancelled the think-timer ~120ms before it could ever fire — the clock counted down, the board froze, and the HUD said 'Thinking...', with zero console errors behind 202 green tests. Both mutation-verified, as is host-transfer promotion.

games.name: sporeline
games.description: Open-field maze tower-defence with a versus twist: there is NO fixed path, and every tower is also a wall, so placement is never 'where does this shoot best' but 'how much longer can I make the road'. The classic rule holds — a placement that would leave any Gate with no route to the Heart is rejected — so you are always shaping a corridor, never closing one. Kills pay gold, split between turtling a tighter maze and SENDING a spore into a rival's field, which drops a creep on them AND permanently raises your own income. Live P2P 2-4 with an unusual netcode: each peer simulates ONLY its own field, so there is no shared continuous state and the classic desync surface does not exist; the host owns exactly three things (wave clock, round seed, win check), which is also the complete list of what a promoted peer takes over. Three modes that change the SHAPE (Skirmish 9x13/one gate, Tangle 7x15 narrow-and-fast, Siege 11x13 with TWO gates on one budget). The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design four times: (1) the game had NO EARLY GAME and no outcome metric could see it — across 240 matches nobody lost a Heart beat before wave 15, and the leader-wins metric reported n=0 rather than a problem, so three quarters of every match was a formality; fixed by hp0 16->42 (average first leak wave 15.1 -> 3.3). (2) The obvious fix for (1) — relaxing the late hp curve — produced BYTE-IDENTICAL early-game numbers and merely stretched matches from 20 to 30 waves, a longer formality. (3) The SIGNATURE MECHANIC NEVER FIRED, revealed only because seven different send economies measured byte-identical in 2P: identical to the digit meant no spore was ever sent in any of them, because the bots' gold reserve made 'balanced' a strict synonym for 'turtle' — two of three policies had been the same policy for every measurement taken until then. (4) A seat bias that REPLICATED ACROSS THREE SEED FAMILIES (Siege 4P 29%/18% vs 25% chance, n=360) was an artifact of the INSTRUMENT: re-running with all four seats on one policy moved the bias around and reversed it, because the families shared an identical seat-to-policy rotation schedule and so were not independent in the dimension being tested. A fix had already been written for it and measured as changing precisely nothing, which is what prompted the check. A real bug it did catch: a lowest-index tie-break in the bot's target selection aimed every opening spore at seat 0. Two further defects were caught by the in-browser pass and by nothing else: INPUT GEOMETRY DEPENDED ON A RENDERED FRAME (cell size was computed only inside the rAF draw, so before the first frame — or in any throttled/hidden tab — cellAt returned -1 and every tap was silently dropped, with no toast, no error and a normal-looking board; it hid from automation because any check that screenshots first fires a frame and makes it vanish), and a KNOCKED-OUT PLAYER WAS LEFT ON A DEAD BOARD for ~6 minutes because solo play only ended when one seat remained. Both mutation-verified, as is the targeting invariant (nearest-to-tower targeting scores 80,407 mis-targeted shots against the shipped 0).
games.status_note: Verified in-browser on the LIVE production URL at a true 375x812 for EVERY mode (Skirmish 9x13 at 39px cells, Tangle 7x15 at 41px, Siege 11x13 at 32px): towers placed successfully in each WITHOUT an intervening repaint (the exact condition that exposed the input-geometry bug), zero horizontal overflow, play surface top-most at the board centre, no stray [hidden] layer, footer hidden mid-round, min tap target 44px, zero console errors. Real-pixel contrast probe on the rendered canvas agrees with the palette test: road 3.47:1, tower 9.15:1, gate 7.03:1, heart 5.66:1 against an empty cell. Full two-peer P2P smoke test passed against the byte-identical local build: create-room plus a typed LOWER-CASE code with stray spaces resolving to the same room, host stickiness (creator kept HOST when the guest joined; guest never showed a host badge), in-sync play, a spore crossing the wire (host '1 sent' / guest '1 taken' on the results table), rematch into a fresh round with both peers seeing each other and the match tally carried, and host-leave -> survivor promoted at EPOCH 2 and reaching the summary rather than a frozen board. TLS cert issued promptly (the Pages CNAME was set ONCE and deliberately not cycled). Known limitation, flagged not hidden: Siege's 11 columns force 32px cells at 375px width, below the 44px ideal — geometrically unavoidable on a phone; every CONTROL is 44px.

games.name: lastlight
games.description: Frozen-frontier settlement game built on one legible rule: only what sits INSIDE the generator's ring of warmth works at all. Hearth-homes shelter nobody outside it, gathering huts must sit ON the tile they harvest, and workshops buff only the neighbours they physically touch — so the ring is a budget drawn in SPACE, and every placement is a bet on where the warmth will still be in three nights. A scout is a worker who is not working, and the survivors they bring home are mouths as well as hands. Solo-first; multiplayer is a PARALLEL SAME-SEED RACE (2-6) where each peer sims only its own settlement, so there is structurally nothing to desync — the host owns seed, countdown and ladder and nothing else, which makes host transfer a display concern rather than a state one. Three modes that change the SHAPE of a run: Thaw (r3/9 nights/ring 2), Frontier (r4/10/ring 2), Longnight (r4/12/ring ONE — you cannot spread, you buy every tile with coal). The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design repeatedly and MOST CONFIDENT DIAGNOSES WERE WRONG: (1) Longnight measured as a warmth-first monoculture at 78/37/42 across the three strategy lines, and the obvious fix — making a big ring cost more heat to hold — moved it only to 77/36/48; instrumenting actual DEATHS instead of reasoning found the real cause, that resources were guaranteed within the starting VISION while Longnight can only build within radius ONE, so the water meant to feed you was visible, known about and unreachable, and runs starved on night 4 with no counterplay that existed on night 1. Anchoring the guarantee to the opening RING gave 79/75/65. (2) Two apparent 'weak strategy' findings were BOT INCOMPETENCE, not game design, and 'fixing' the game would have been actively wrong: an expand-first bot with a fixed small ring built outward then let its own huts freeze, and no bot ever hired idle survivors, which made exploration's headline payoff a pure liability. (3) Making the game HARSHER made the strongest line STRONGER (expand 88->92%), because scarcer food punishes the line that gains population and leaves untouched the line that never does. Numbers replicated across three independent 200-run seed families (each line varying only 2-6 points), so unlike an earlier game in this fleet these are signal rather than +/-8 noise. Final: Thaw 85/86/70, Frontier 83/87/72, Longnight 76/82/63; ringCost pinned by its own test. Mechanism invariants (principle #21) audit every night from OUTSIDE the sim against the BOARD at zero tolerance — shelter, production, staffing, starvation, scouts and a full resource ledger — 0 violations across all 9 mode x strategy cells; debugging the first failures found a bug in the AUDIT (a scout dispatched during today's day phase leaves the workforce before production runs), which is precisely why an audit must be debugged rather than assumed. The contrast gate also proved a fill-only 'frozen' state is IMPOSSIBLE (frozen forest measured 1.03:1 against lit forest — literally no change — while frozen snow managed 1.48:1), so a dead tile is marked by a FROST OVERLAY held to the same 3:1 mark floor. Two real defects were caught only by the in-browser pass: the map rendered 188px tall in a 630px stage because a canvas carries an intrinsic aspect ratio from its backing store and `height:100%` against a flex-grown parent silently resolved to auto; and every mode button rendered in the BROWSER'S DEFAULT GREY because the button helper REPLACED its default class instead of merging, so those buttons carried no .btn rule and the contrast test stayed green — the palette was never what painted them. Both mutation-verified.
games.status_note: Verified in a real browser on the LIVE production https URL at a true 375x812 for ALL THREE modes (Thaw 1/9 ring 2, Frontier 1/10 ring 2, Longnight 1/12 ring 1): zero horizontal overflow, canvas is the top element at board centre, no stray [hidden] layer, all tap targets >=44px, footer hidden mid-round, canvas filling the 628px stage, zero console errors. Solo play verified end-to-end locally through to the results screen. The mandatory contrast PIXEL PROBE was run on the real canvas, not just the palette: generator 3.16:1 and gathering hut 5.89:1 against the tiles they are painted on, with terrain pairs holding ~1.3:1 even under the warm gradient. NOT live-verified: the two-peer P2P smoke test (typed-code join, in-sync standings, host-leave, rematch) — two live browser contexts were not run against each other, so that contract is covered by automated tests instead: takeover.test.ts (9 tests, MUTATION-VERIFIED — disabling promotion turns it red), host-election.test.ts, net-lifecycle.test.ts, rematch.test.ts and no-deadlock.test.ts. 157 tests total across 13 files. Note for future runs: curl could not reach the domain from this machine because macOS had negatively cached the hostname from before the DNS record existed (dig resolved it fine, getaddrinfo did not); the browser resolves independently, which is what made the production pass possible.

games.name: turntide
games.description: Draughts with one rule replaced: a jump CONVERTS instead of capturing. You leap an adjacent enemy, land beyond it, and the jumped stone stays exactly where it is and turns your colour — so all 24 stones remain on the board for the whole game and material CIRCULATES rather than accumulating. Every jump is a two-stone swing, and the board is as crowded at the finish as at the start. Three rules keep that from being noise: crowns cannot be farmed (a flipped king is demoted to a man), cooling (a just-converted stone cannot be flipped back until it has sat through an enemy turn), and the rout (control 18 of 24 and you win instantly, so a genuine collapse ends rather than dribbling to an even split). Solo vs a 3-strength alpha-beta AI; live 2-player P2P that is LOCKSTEP rather than a snapshot star — zero randomness and zero hidden info mean peers exchange only {from,path} and the boards structurally cannot desync, leaving the turn CLOCK as the single host-authoritative thing (which is all host transfer takes over). FIRST GAME BUILT ON THE EXTRACTED ENGINE PACKAGE @ben-gy/game-engine v1.1.0 — patterns/ was deleted mid-run and the game depends on the package rather than copying source. The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design three times: (1) the per-turn FLIP CAP, the plan's headline balance lever, is not a lever at all — caps of 3/4/5/6 gave byte-identical results because with forward-only jumps a chain runs out of board after ~3 links, so Floodmark's 'one chain can swing ten stones' was false copy for a mechanic that never fires; the real cascade lever turned out to be MEN JUMPING BACKWARD (big chains 2%->8%, the only setting where the cap ever binds), confined to the 10x10 because on the 8x8 it took blowouts 33%->63%. (2) COOLING was to be doubled in one mode to make it positional; the cooling-0 control arm showed it barely moves any outcome metric (its real job is damping repetition, 318->250 repeats), and cooling 2 pushed Skirmish's FIRST-PLAYER win rate to 66% — a flavour rule silently breaking seat fairness elsewhere. (3) A seat bias nearly got designed around: the same Skirmish config read 41%, 49% and 54% across three seed families at n~140 (95% CI +/-8), so the early 37%/43% readings meant nothing; raising n to 400 and replicating showed Tidepool's 6x6 edge WAS real (39.9/40.4/40.8%) while Skirmish's was not, and of six candidate fixes only giving Tidepool the full 8x8 worked (49.2/50.3%, blowouts 49%->22%). The 6x6 board was cut. Also measured: greedy chain-grabbing LOSES (0-13% vs a searching bot), so the snowball the design feared does not exist. Two real defects were found by gates unit tests could not see: the contrast test caught that the two player colours sat at a 1.06:1 LUMINANCE ratio — the palette's own colour-blind-safety claim was false and the sides were the same shade of grey — and the live two-peer smoke test caught that THREE of the engine's six curated signaling relays were impaired at once (nostr.wine write-restricted, damus rate-limited, nostr.band unresponsive), so peers landed on non-overlapping working subsets and never met.
games.status_note: Deploy succeeded and production serves correctly over HTTP: 200 with the correct <title>, every asset 200 (HTML, JS, CSS, manifest, all four icons, og, robots, sitemap, IndexNow key), and the shipped JS AND CSS are a byte-for-byte SHA256 match for the exact local build that was fully play-verified in a real browser at a true 375x812 across ALL THREE modes (Tidepool 8x8 and Skirmish 8x8 at 43px cells, Floodmark 10x10 at 34px): zero horizontal overflow, zero cell overlaps, play surface top-most at the board centre, no stray [hidden] layer, footer hidden mid-round, zero console errors. The signature mechanic was verified end-to-end in-browser (a jump took the tide bar 12/12 -> 11/13, a two-stone swing, with the flip animation and the cooling rim+notch cue rendering correctly). Floodmark's 34px cells are below the 44px ideal and geometrically forced (10 columns at 375px); flagged, not hidden. NOT marked deployed because GitHub had not issued the TLS cert for the custom domain (the Pages API reports no https_certificate object at all after ~15 min; DNS resolves correctly), and both browser surfaces refuse plain HTTP, so a visual pass against the live https URL could not be taken. The Pages CNAME was set ONCE and deliberately NOT cycled, per the emberwake lesson that cycling requeues issuance. P2P is PARTIALLY live-verified: mesh formation (2/2), typed lower-case code join resolving to the same room, roster and ready-vote propagation, host stickiness (the creator kept HOST when the joiner arrived; the joiner never showed a host badge), no host badge before settle, and peer-leave landing the survivor on a full per-player results screen ALL passed live. NOT live-verified: in-sync move exchange, host-leave takeover, and rematch - sandbox relay discovery was too flaky to hold a mesh (it formed once, then would not re-form), traced to THREE of the engine's six curated relays being impaired at once and aggravated by ~15 concurrent factory sessions on one IP plus background-tab throttling. Those three are covered by automated tests: takeover.test.ts (9 tests, MUTATION-VERIFIED - disabling promotion turns it red), rematch.test.ts, no-deadlock.test.ts, host-election.test.ts, net-lifecycle.test.ts. 191 tests total. Flip to deployed once https://turntide.benrichardson.dev serves 200 and a ~375px per-mode screenshot is taken.

games.name: changeling
games.description: Real chess — standard pieces, castling, en passant, promotion, win by checkmate — with ONE rule bolted on: the instant a piece captures, it becomes the TYPE of what it captured. The king is immune, so checkmate still means what it always meant. Promotion COMPOSES with the morph rather than being special-cased (a rook that eats a pawn on the last rank becomes a pawn AND promotes, so the generator offers the choice), and the base generator is pinned against published chess perft (20/400/8902 for the first three plies, which the morph provably cannot change). The design consequence is that recapture math inverts: capturing with your best piece downgrades it, so whoever recaptures LAST holds the good shape and initiating an exchange is a commitment. Material stops accumulating and starts CIRCULATING, which the material 'tide' bar makes the primary feedback. Solo vs a 3-strength alpha-beta engine; live 2-player P2P that is LOCKSTEP rather than a snapshot star — zero randomness and zero hidden info mean peers exchange only {from,to,promo} and the boards structurally cannot desync, leaving the CLOCK as the single host-authoritative thing (which is what host transfer actually takes over). Three modes: Classic 8x8, Skirmish 6x6 knife-fight (no castling, pawns walk), Wildcourt 8x8 with a seeded back-rank shuffle IDENTICAL for both sides so the opening is fair by construction. The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design twice: (1) the first measurement LIED — raw material said an early leader wins only ~27%, an artifact of reading material mid-exchange, which systematically favours the side about to be recaptured; resolving pending captures first (quietEval) made the question real, and believing the raw number would have aimed a fix at a snowball that does not exist. (2) White's first-move edge does NOT survive the morph — 260 paired games of Classic give White 46.5% where chess gives ~55%, because moving first means more often INITIATING the exchange you then get punished for — so no compensation was added. Three real bugs were caught by gates that unit tests could not see: both peers were ejected to the menu the instant the second joined (the net handlers' repaint fell back to showMenu with no live round — multiplayer 100% broken while solo play, tsc and 130 tests were all green); the host leaving stranded the survivor on a dead board (peer-leave ended the round and showed the summary, then the same handler repainted the board over it); and pieces were stranded a cell off their square in a backgrounded tab (the slide released its offset in a requestAnimationFrame, which a hidden tab never fires). All three mutation-verified.
games.status_note: Both deploys succeeded and production serves correctly over HTTP (200, correct <title>), with the shipped JS and CSS a byte-for-byte SHA256 match for the exact local build that was fully play-verified in a real browser at a true 375x812 across ALL THREE modes (Classic 8x8 44px cells, Skirmish 6x6 59px cells, Wildcourt 8x8 with the shuffled back rank confirmed identical for both sides in the live DOM) — zero horizontal overflow, play surface top-most at the board centre, no stray [hidden] layer, zero console errors. The full two-peer P2P smoke test also passed against that build: typed lower-case code join, in-sync play both ways, host stickiness, the host's mode travelling to a guest who had picked a different one locally, host-leave -> survivor promoted and reaching the summary, and rematch -> both peers in a fresh round with colours swapped and the mesh still live. NOT marked deployed because GitHub had not issued the TLS cert for the custom domain after ~35 minutes (the Pages API reports no https_certificate object at all; DNS resolves correctly and matches a working sibling), and both browser surfaces refuse plain HTTP, so a visual pass against the live https URL could not be taken. The Pages CNAME was set ONCE and deliberately NOT cycled, per the emberwake lesson that cycling requeues issuance. Flip to deployed once https://changeling.benrichardson.dev serves 200 and a ~375px per-mode screenshot is taken.

games.name: frostward
games.description: Simultaneous-commit board game for 2-4 built on one legible rule: the Rime spreads ONLY into the Gale's forward arc, so it grows as a cone that sweeps like a searchlight as the wind swings. Each turn everyone secretly plays one card — Veer (everyone's veers that turn SUM, so the wind is a tug-of-war), Ridge, Thaw, or Ember (plant a brand-new cone anywhere) — then the Rime steps and any hearth it reaches beats it back once before going dark for good. The tension is structural: the only way to get the cone off your hearths is to point it at somebody else's, and a turn spent bending the wind is a turn not spent walling. Live P2P where NO board state is ever transmitted — a round is a pure function of (seed, mode, roster, commits), so peers exchange only commits, which makes host transfer a state-wise no-op (the promoted peer already ran the identical sim) and a late joiner a few kB of replay. The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design repeatedly: the first build's full 135deg arc was nine cells wide against a three-cell hearth cluster, so median games ran 3-10 turns, leader-at-turn-2 held 70-81%, and the winner finished untouched >50% of the time. Fixes that MEASURED: a flank rule (frost advances straight down the wind but only creeps sideways into a cell already half-surrounded — small early, big late, with zero new state), a hearth guard + rime delay (which flattened 4P seats from 20/22/23/35 to 26/24/26/24 — the seat bias was NOT geometric, which is what everyone would have guessed), and fanning hearths across the sector (2P blowouts 57%->34%). Refuted: hearthGuard 2 (longer games, worse blowouts), rimeDelay 2, and lowering Whiteout's turn cap (converted kills into shrugs; stepping every 2nd turn instead is what shortened it). Most instructive, driftHunt was written into the plan as THE anti-snowball lever and measured as barely moving the leader curve at all — it is a pacing lever, and the test now pins that description so nobody re-derives the original story. Three modes that change the round (Drift 11x11, Whiteout 13x13 double-stepping, Emberfall 9x9 ember-heavy hand of 4). Two real defects were found by the in-browser two-tab pass rather than any unit test: a guest could see nobody ready ever (only the host receives commits) and 'Change my card' never told the host, so it would resolve the withdrawn card.

games.name: deepwatch
games.description: Silent co-op card game of nerve for 2-4 divers: surface a hand of deep-sea creatures onto one shared line from shallowest to deepest, where NO number appears anywhere (a card's height on the depth gauge IS its depth) and there is no chat channel by design — the only information is who hasn't played yet and how long they've been not-playing. A misplay dredges up every shallower card and burns one of a few SHARED air tanks. The signature twist is the sonar ping, spendable only when every diver holds their card at once. One card, two gestures (tap to surface, press-and-hold to signal) — and a hold can never resolve as a tap, or every signal would surface a card. Live P2P co-op; hands are NEVER transmitted, only derived from (seed, level) minus what has left play, which makes host transfer a no-op state-wise (proven by tests/takeover.test.ts). Three modes: Shallows, Trench, Abyss (crowded deck + you only ever see your next card). A mandatory co-op balance sim OVERRULED five confident diagnoses: the rising tide never fired at all; sonar was WORSE than not having it (7% vs 8%) until divers learned to JOIN each other's signals (+5.35 sigma over 6,300 paired runs); widening the deck did nothing (proportional-only jitter makes every absolute scale cancel); scaling air tanks didn't rescue big crews even at +3/diver; and a broken control arm hid it all (sonar:false zeroed pings once, but modes grant bonus charges at pingAt levels). The real fixes were structural: a tide budget PER CARD, and a dive whose depth scales DOWN as the crew grows so every party faces the same total cards. Crucially the sim also certified a level 1 that ran in 6.3s — a real human got ZERO taps in before the tide surfaced their cards, because the sim's diver judges instantly; the tide was slowed to human speed and JITTER raised until the curve returned. Also found and mutation-guarded a lingering countdown-overlay leak on the live site.
games.status_note: Verified in-browser on the LIVE production URL at true 375x812 for EVERY mode (Shallows/4 -> 5 levels, Trench/4 -> 7, Abyss/4 -> 8): zero horizontal overflow, card fully on-screen and >=44px, four crew chips with no overlap, footer hidden mid-round, no stray [hidden] layer, zero console errors. Two-peer P2P smoke test NOT run against a second live browser context; host transfer is covered by 13 automated takeover tests including 'can still reach game over'.

games.name: emberwake
games.description: Neon lane-runner built on one legible rule: momentum is your speed, your health AND your score at once. A wall of dark chases at a floor speed that rises with distance, so clipping a hazard never kills you outright — it bleeds momentum, you slow, and the dark closes. The design consequence is the whole game: sidestepping into an empty lane is always safe and pays nothing, while THREADING a hazard in-lane (jump the pit you're standing in, slide under the bar) pays the refill you need to afford the next chasm. Chasms are crossable only while SURGING, and surging spends the very momentum keeping you ahead. Swipe controls (lane/jump/slide) + a held SURGE pad — no D-pad. Solo score-attack; async-seed multiplayer via a worldwide UTC daily seed, share links carrying seed+mode+distance, and a translucent ghost of your own best run on that track — all byte-exact because the sim is a pure function of (seed, mode, inputs). Results show a deterministic bot's 'clean line' distance on your exact seed so you know what you left on the table. Three modes with real spread: Ember (3 lanes, balanced), Nightfall (3 lanes, dark starts close and gains fast, mote-sparse), Latticework (5 lanes, denser hazards, gentler dark, longest runs). The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design twice — with no momentum decay the bot pinned momentum at max and cruised 6-9 minutes to a fixed wall with a 3% spread and zero skill differentiation; over-correcting with heavy decay ended every run by starvation BEFORE the first chasm, making the signature surge mechanic irrelevant in 2 of 3 modes and scoring all policies identically. Shipped economy is the third attempt: mixed surge beats pure-hoarding 3.3-4.3x and surge-spam 4.8-7.3x, <=3% chasm fumbles, zero unfair early deaths. Fairness is structural (strictly-increasing event distances => a safe lane always exists, pinned by a test).
games.status_note: Deploy succeeded and production serves correctly over HTTP: every asset 200 (HTML, JS, CSS, manifest, all four icons, og, robots, sitemap), and the shipped JS+CSS are a byte-for-byte SHA256 match for the exact local build that was fully play-verified in-browser at 375px across all three modes. NOT marked deployed because GitHub's TLS cert for the custom domain was still stuck in state 'new' after ~70min (early CNAME cycling likely requeued it), and both browser surfaces refuse plain HTTP, so a visual pass against the LIVE https URL could not be taken. Flip to deployed once https://emberwake.benrichardson.dev serves 200 and a ~375px per-mode screenshot is taken.

games.name: scrapwall
games.description: Co-op grid base-defence: wall a central Core from shared scrap, feed auto-turrets the ammo you harvest from renewable salvage nodes, and hold an ever-worsening horde that pathfinds to the Core along the path of LEAST RESISTANCE — a weighted-Dijkstra flow-field routes husks around strong walls and digs through your weakest one (a real routing bug was found here — the flow-field weighted the source cell instead of the entered cell, luring husks INTO walls — and mutation-guarded). Over-fortifying starves the guns of the shared ammo pool, so the fort is a budget not a wish-list. Tap-to-build, drag-to-paint a wall run, tap-to-harvest — a board-game control scheme, no D-pad. Solo-first; live P2P co-op for 2-4 (host-authoritative snapshot star, actions on 'act', full grid+horde snapshot at 10Hz); host transfer promotes a survivor who rebuilds the deterministic spawn queue and keeps the run finishable. Three modes that change the SPACE not a dial: Outpost 9x9/two edges, Depot 11x11/four edges, Sprawl 13x13/four edges. Co-op scaling is honest — the horde and the scrap income both scale with party count (peer leaving shrinks the horde), refereed by a mandatory balance sim (AI-vs-the-horde over hundreds of seeds): a smooth wave ramp with a contested middle, every run terminates (the immortal-turret-carpet trap was caught + fixed), party playable at every size, RAMP_EXP pinned as the load-bearing termination constant. Full two-peer smoke test passed on live production: typed-code join, in-sync co-op play, host-leave -> survivor keeps playing, rematch into a fresh shared round.

games.name: tiny-tanks
games.description: Top-down two-team capture-the-flag tank duel: your shots RICOCHET off the walls (bank them around corners; your own rebound can kill you), steal the enemy flag and race it home, first team to 3 wins. Two-hit tanks + a grab-invuln make a flag run a real, winnable race; nobody is eliminated (respawn); power-ups drop mid-field (spread, rapid, speed, shield, extra ricochet). Always a 2v2 with bots backfilling empty seats, so solo (you + a bot ally vs two bots) and 2-player both feel full. Live P2P versus (2-4 humans), host-authoritative snapshot star, host transfer promotes a survivor, rematch inside the room with a running match tally. Twin floating analog sticks on touch (left drives, right aims + auto-fires); WASD + mouse on desktop. PORTRAIT arena (bases top/bottom) so it fills a phone. Three modes that change the SPATIAL problem (Clash open, Labyrinth dense cover, Rampart structured chokes). The sim is PROVABLY point-symmetric (a mirror-input fairness test keeps seats exactly mirrored) so team fairness is by construction; the mandatory balance sim (48-55% team rate, P(lead@1) 66-72%) OVERRULED the design repeatedly — one-shot kills gave ZERO bot captures, fixed with two-hit tanks + grab-invuln + return-path cover, and it caught a self-symmetric centre pickup the lower seat always won (removed).

games.name: grainfall
games.description: Hammurabi-style micro-civilisation economy sim: rule a city-state one year at a time, splitting your grain three ways — trade land at a price that swings each year, sow the fields (a bushel of seed per acre, a farmer works 10), and feed your people (20 bushels each) — then the year rolls: harvest (weather sets bushels/acre), rats, plague, and settlers. Lose >45% of your people to starvation in a year and you're deposed. Solo-first with async seed-share (Daily Reign + challenge links replay a byte-identical deterministic decade; results screen shows a masterful-steward benchmark on your exact seed). Three genuinely different reigns: Steward (10y balanced), Famine (11y defensive attrition, break-even economy), Dynasty (18y boom + land speculation). The economy IS the game: a balance sim (cautious vs greedy bots, 400 seeds/mode) OVERRULED the design twice — Famine was mathematically unwinnable (net food per farmed person < feed need) and Dynasty's blind auto-growth swelled the city past its land's feeding capacity; fixed with break-even tuning + a settler land-cap (mutation-verified). A ~375px mobile HUD number-clipping bug was caught in the per-mode browser pass and pinned with a CSS-invariant guard.

games.name: gloamrun
games.description: Endless co-op dungeon crawler: descend procedurally generated floors against an ever-worsening swarm — you auto-fire at the nearest monster so the game is all in your feet (position, kite, dash with i-frames), clear the floor, step on the stair, draft one of three upgrades, go deeper. Downed players are revivable by a teammate, never eliminated; the run ends only on a party wipe, and clearing a floor revives the party (floors are checkpoints). Fully playable solo; live P2P co-op for 2-4 over a shared room code. Host-authoritative star (host runs monster sim + floor machine and broadcasts a full snapshot, clients send input and predict their own hero); host transfer promotes a survivor who keeps the run finishable. Three modes with real spread (Delve roomy, Onslaught crowds, Gauntlet tight elites), boss every few floors. The difficulty curve is the opponent: a mandatory sim (tests/balance.test.ts) refereed the ramp and caught a 4-player immortal-party bug (out-DPSing a polynomial ramp forever) — fixed with a geometric HP term past floor 5 that guarantees termination. Also back-ported the stale rematch `cur` catch-up fix into patterns/.

games.name: delvepack
games.description: Co-op top-down dungeon crawler: descend procedurally-generated floors, auto-fire at the nearest monster, dash to dodge (i-frames), clear a floor to drop upgrade orbs and light the exit rune, then stand on it to descend — the ramp is the whole difficulty. Solo score-attack (deepest floor) plus live P2P for 2-4 delvers in the SAME deterministic dungeon against the dungeon, reviving downed teammates over a bleed-out window (all-down = wipe; a dropped peer just makes the run harder). Host-authoritative snapshot star: host owns monsters/bolts/HP/floor/rune, each peer owns only its own delver's motion; host transfer promotes a survivor who rebuilds combat stats from the deterministic formula and keeps the run finishable. Three modes that change the SPACE not a dial (Delve mid-hall, Warren wide crowd-cavern, Crypt tight knife-fight). NOTE: built concurrently with (and independent of) ben-gy/gloamrun from the same IDEAS.md line — a shared-queue race; both shipped. The co-op difficulty curve is refereed by an AI-vs-the-dungeon balance sim that OVERRULED the design (crowds+revive-cascades made co-op a tax, not deeper) and surfaced two real softlocks (a monster unkillable behind a pillar; an orb-fixation stall), both fixed and mutation-guarded.

games.name: morsel
games.description: Petri-dish free-for-all: eat pellets to grow, swallow any smaller blob whole, run from bigger ones. Big blobs are slow, so the only way to catch anyone is to DASH — and a dash costs 15% of your mass, sprayed out behind you as food for whoever you're chasing, so the only way to grow your lead is to spend it. Pellets ramp in value over the round (the bloom) so an early lead banks nothing and the last stretch decides it; being swallowed just costs 1.5s and nobody is ever out. Three modes (Skirmish tiny/6/110s, Petri medium/6/150s, Famine big/8/180s with a third of the food); solo vs bots, live P2P free-for-all for 2-8, or an async seed-share. Split authority: each peer owns its own blob's motion and death, the host owns the pellet field, bots, clock and mass ledger; host transfer promotes a survivor who keeps the round finishable. Balance simulated AI-vs-AI (P(leader wins) flat early then spikes, every seat within a few points of chance) — the design doc predicted a snowball; the sim proved the opposite and drove the fix.

games.name: driftlock
games.description: Abstract board game on a wrapping grid where you never touch a stone — you shift the whole row or column it stands on, everything slides and wraps, and any stone that lands in the central Well banks for whoever made the shift (stones are neutral, so there is no seat geometry to make unfair). The line you just shifted is locked for your opponent's next turn, so the game is about leaving them nothing to take. The Well rises as a pure function of the move number and stones are worth more the later they land, so an early lead is not a won game — a design the balance sim refereed and overruled repeatedly (documented in src/game.ts). Solo vs 3-strength bots, live P2P for 2, and async seed-share; three modes (Blitz 5-stone, Duel 9-stone, Deepwell 9x9).

games.name: windup
games.description: Simultaneous-programming strategy: slot three movement cards into your wind-up bot, then every bot's program executes at once, one step at a time — and bots shove each other, so where you end up is rarely where you planned. Cogs ripen (+1 value a round, up to 5) so the board starts cheap and ends enormous, and everyone converges on the fat centre cog at the climax. Falling in a pit costs tempo, never elimination. Three modes (Skirmish 9x9/8 rounds, Clockwork 11x11/12, Gauntlet 13x13/16 with four program slots); solo vs bots, live P2P for 2-4, or an async seed-share challenge. Board is 90-degree rotationally symmetric with cogs spawning in symmetric orbits, so turn-0 is provably fair.
games.status_note: Deploy succeeded and every production asset serves 200 (HTML, JS, CSS, manifest, all four icons), with the [hidden] guard confirmed in the shipped CSS. Full in-browser play + two-peer P2P smoke test (typed-code join, in-sync play, host stickiness, host-leave -> survivor finishes) all passed against the byte-identical local build of this commit. NOT marked deployed because the browser tooling (Chrome extension + in-app pane) went down before a visual pass against the live URL and a ~375px mobile screenshot could be taken.

games.name: bidstorm
games.description: Bidding card game with no luck in the deal: every player holds the identical hand 1-13 and secretly commits one card per flipped prize, highest bid takes the pot and every card played is burned forever, while a tie for the lead wins nothing and carries the pot into the next round. Every hand is public, so it's a decision rather than a guess. Three modes (Classic, Foresight's two-prize lookahead, Blitz's seven cards where a tie burns the prize); solo vs bots, live P2P for 2-6, or an async seed-share challenge.

games.name: lumenlock
games.description: Light-routing puzzle: a white beam hits a prism and fans into a rainbow (red bends left, green straight, blue right); flip mirrors and half-silvered splitters to lock every crystal in its exact colour, where a crystal sums everything it receives so a stray beam spoils it and a white crystal takes either untouched white light or red+green+blue recombined. Solo, with an exhaustively-searched true par, a UTC Daily Lock and seeded share links.

games.name: nightwire
games.description: Hidden-role social deduction on a ring of seats: each night you probe a seat and learn how many Ghosts sit in that 3-seat window, but while Crew readings publish automatically and truthfully, Ghosts choose what number to publish — eject every Ghost before the nightly wire cuts cause a blackout; solo vs solver bots or live P2P for 4-10 humans.

games.name: orbital-skirmish
games.description: Momentum dogfight in a circular arena with a lethal star at the centre that pulls ships, bullets AND rocks — so you never aim where a rival is, you aim where gravity will carry the shot, and a bullet inherits your velocity so charging is brutal and fleeing is feeble. Solo Survival (escalating AI waves, five lives, instant play) plus live P2P deathmatch for 2–4, host-authoritative snapshot star, rematch inside the room with a running tally. Three modes that change the spatial problem not a dial: Skirmish (open arena), Nova (the star flares on a nine-second clock), Belt (weak star, dense rock). Spawns sit on a rotationally symmetric ring so no seat starts ahead — a fairness the mandatory balance sim measured rather than assumed (it found two real bugs and refuted two confident diagnoses; see src/modes.ts and tests/balance.test.ts). Finishes the 2026-07-13 slot the original run abandoned after 70 seconds.

games.name: gravity-golf
games.description: Slingshot physics mini-golf: pull back to launch and bend your ball around gravity wells (attractors pull, repulsors push, black holes swallow) into the hole across 9 procedural holes; solo, async seed-share, or live P2P race up to 6 players.

games.name: snake-royale
games.description: Classic snake in a shared arena: eat pellets to grow and score, avoid walls/snakes/your own tail; solo Endless score-attack plus live P2P Royale where the last snake alive wins.

games.name: rhythm-relay
games.description: Two-lane rhythm game: notes relay left↔right toward a hit line; tap in time to build a shared combo and score multiplier, misses drain energy; solo or take a lane each in live P2P co-op.

games.name: hexbloom
games.description: Turn-based hex-grid area control: pick a colour to recolour your territory and absorb every touching tile of it; largest territory when the board fills wins.

games.name: cipher-clash
games.description: Boggle-style word-grid race; drag touching letters to spell words, and in versus a word only scores for whoever claims it first.
